projects.title: Unit 7 Project — Equations & Inequalities in Action
projects.desc: Capstone project applying writing and solving equations and inequalities to real situations.

projects.title: Neon Inequality Gatekeeper
projects.desc: Graph inequalities on a number line to clear each neon gate.

title: Graph Inequalities
contentObjective: I can graph the solutions of an inequality on a number line.
languageObjective: I can explain my graph using the words number line, open circle, closed circle, and solution set.

turnAndTalk.question: Detective Okafor knows the suspect arrived 'no earlier than 2:00 PM,' so t ≥ 2. On the timeline, should the mark at 2 be an open or closed circle, and which way should she shade?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Because 2:00 itself counts, I use a closed circle at 2.

stems.en: I use a ___ circle at 2 because 2:00 ___ included.
stems.es: Uso un círculo ___ en 2 porque las 2:00 ___ incluidas.

stems.en: I shade ___ because the times can be ___.
stems.es: Sombreo ___ porque las horas pueden ser ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students choose a closed circle at 2 (because 'no earlier than' includes 2) and shade to the right since later times satisfy t ≥ 2.
turnAndTalk.extend: How would the timeline look different if the clue were t > 2 instead of t ≥ 2? Justify the change in the circle.
extendStems: For t > 2 I would use a ___ circle because ___.
The boundary value 2 would ___ in that case.

turnAndTalk.question: When you graphed x > 4 and x ≤ 7, why did you use an open circle on one and a closed circle on the other?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Open circles exclude the boundary; closed circles include it.

stems.en: I used a ___ circle because the symbol ___ means the boundary is ___.
stems.es: Usé un círculo ___ porque el símbolo ___ significa que el límite ___.

stems.en: I shaded ___ because the solution set includes ___.
stems.es: Sombreé ___ porque el conjunto solución incluye ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students connect > and < to open circles (boundary not included) and ≥ and ≤ to closed circles, and shade toward the values in the solution set.
turnAndTalk.extend: Why does the graph of x > 4 represent infinitely many solutions even though it starts at one point? Explain using the solution set.
extendStems: The graph shows infinitely many solutions because ___.
Every point in the shaded part is ___.

turnAndTalk.question: When would graphing an inequality show a real-life limit better than a single number?
turnAndTalk.kernel: A graph shows every value that fits a rule, not just one.

stems.en: A graph shows a real limit like ___ because ___.
stems.es: Una gráfica muestra un límite real como ___ porque ___.

stems.en: The ___ circle shows whether the limit value ___ allowed.
stems.es: El círculo ___ muestra si el valor límite ___ permitido.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students name a real limit (minimum age, max weight, earliest time) and explain the circle type shows whether the boundary value is allowed.
turnAndTalk.extend: Critique: 'An open circle and a closed circle graph the same solution set.' Use x > 5 and x ≥ 5 to show why this is wrong.
extendStems: This is wrong because x ≥ 5 includes ___ but x > 5 ___.
The two solution sets differ by ___.

turnAndTalk.question: During practice on Graph Inequalities, what strategy did you use when a problem felt tricky?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Closed circle for ≤ or ≥ (included), open circle for < or > (not included); shade right for greater, left for less.

stems.en: I used a ___ circle because the symbol ___ means the boundary is ___.
stems.es: Usé un círculo ___ porque el símbolo ___ significa que el límite ___.

stems.en: I knew my answer was reasonable because ___.
stems.es: Supe que mi respuesta era razonable porque ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Listen for students naming a specific strategy tied to 6.EE.8 — not just "I multiplied." They should connect steps to the key idea.
turnAndTalk.extend: How would you help a classmate who made the common mistake from today's lesson? Use one vocabulary word.
extendStems: I would tell them to ___ first because ___.
The mistake happens when you ___ instead of ___.

vocabulary.definition: A line with numbers in order. You use it to show answers.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Una línea con números en orden. La usas para mostrar respuestas.
vocabulary.visual: A horizontal line with marks at 0, 1, 2, 3, ... showing where values fall
vocabulary.cloze: A line with numbers in order used to show values and solutions is a ___.

vocabulary.definition: An empty circle showing a number is NOT included.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Un círculo vacío que muestra que un número NO está incluido.
vocabulary.visual: x > 5: open circle at 5 means 5 itself is NOT a solution, but 5.1, 6, 7, ... are
vocabulary.cloze: A hollow circle that shows a value is NOT included, used for < or >, is an ___.

examples.why: 3 is not included when the symbol is >.
≥ includes 3, so it needs a closed circle.

vocabulary.definition: A filled-in circle showing a number IS included.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Un círculo relleno que muestra que un número SÍ está incluido.
vocabulary.visual: x ≥ 5: closed circle at 5 means 5 IS a solution, and so are 6, 7, 8, ...
vocabulary.cloze: A filled-in circle that shows a value IS included, used for ≤ or ≥, is a ___.

vocabulary.definition: All the numbers that make the inequality true.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Todos los números que hacen verdadera la desigualdad.
vocabulary.visual: For x > 3, the solution set includes 3.1, 4, 5, 10, 100 — any number greater than 3
vocabulary.cloze: All the values that make an inequality true form the ___.

vocabulary.definition: A math sentence comparing two amounts with <, >, ≤, or ≥.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Una oración matemática que compara dos cantidades con <, >, ≤ o ≥.
vocabulary.visual: x ≥ 3 is an inequality; it is graphed as a closed circle on 3 with an arrow to the right.
vocabulary.cloze: A math sentence comparing two amounts with <, >, ≤, or ≥ is an ___.

vocabulary.definition: The number on the number line where the solution set starts.
vocabulary.definitionEs: El número en la recta numérica donde comienza el conjunto solución.
vocabulary.visual: For x > 5, the boundary point is 5, shown with an open circle.
vocabulary.cloze: The number where the solution set begins on the number line is the ___.

launch.narrative: Detective Okafor is building a timeline. The suspect was seen at the scene no earlier than 2:00 PM, meaning the arrival time t satisfies t ≥ 2. She needs to plot this on a timeline to share with her team. How should she mark the number line?
launch.contextImage: Detective pointing at a large wall timeline with pushpins and string connecting events
noticePrompts: What does 'no earlier than 2:00 PM' mean about the time?
Which direction on the number line would you shade?
wonderPrompts: What would the graph look like if the suspect arrived before 2:00 PM?
How would you show the difference between t > 2 and t ≥ 2 on a number line?

conceptIntro.heading: How do I graph an inequality on a number line?
conceptIntro.intro: Graphing an inequality shows every value that makes it true. I draw a circle at the boundary number and shade the side that works. The circle tells me if the boundary is included.
conceptIntro.keyIdea: Closed circle for ≤ or ≥ (included), open circle for < or > (not included); shade right for greater, left for less.

iDo.title: Watch me
lines: Graph t ≥ 2. The boundary number is 2.
The symbol ≥ includes 2, so I draw a closed (filled) circle at 2.
'Greater than or equal to' means larger values work, so I shade to the right.
Now the graph shows 2, 3, 4, and every value above 2.

weDo.title: Let's try together
lines: Graph x < 5. What number is the boundary?
The symbol < does NOT include 5, so we use an open (empty) circle at 5.
Which way do we shade for 'less than'? To the left, toward smaller numbers.

youDo.title: Now it's your turn
lines: Graph x > 4, x ≤ 7, and x ≥ 2 by choosing the right circle and shading.
Then complete the practice graphs on your own.

explore.instructions: Graph each inequality on the number line. Decide whether to use an open or closed circle, then shade in the correct direction.
hints: Use an open circle for < and > (the boundary is NOT included). Use a closed circle for ≤ and ≥ (the boundary IS included).
Shade to the RIGHT for > or ≥ (values get larger). Shade to the LEFT for < or ≤ (values get smaller).
explore.label: Graph each inequality. Choose the correct circle and shade the correct direction.

discourse.prompt: How do you decide between an open circle and a closed circle when graphing?
discourse.sentenceFrame: I use a ___ circle when the symbol is ___ because the boundary value ___ part of the solution.

optionalActivity.name: Open Circle / Closed Circle Number Line Lab
optionalActivity.intro: Detective Okafor needs each clue plotted exactly right — pick the correct circle and shade the case wide open.

optional.stem: Clue: the witness ran more than 15 feet, so d > 15. On the number line, what do you draw?
optional.explanation: 'Greater than' (>) is a strict symbol, so 15 is NOT included — open circle. Larger values work, so shade right.

optional.stem: Clue: the getaway car held at most 4 suspects, so s ≤ 4. How is this graphed?
optional.explanation: 'Less than or equal to' (≤) includes 4 — closed circle. Smaller values work, so shade left.

optional.instructions: Detective Okafor is filing clues. Sort each inequality by the circle its graph uses.

optional.label: Match each clue inequality to the correct graph in the case file.

optional.title: Level 2 Extension — Crack the Graphing Mistake

workedExample.label: Clue inequality
workedExample.work: k > 19 (the loot weighed more than 19 pounds)

optional.correctWork: k > 19 uses the strict symbol >, so 19 is NOT a solution. The circle must be OPEN, not closed. The shading right is correct. Final graph: open circle at 19, shaded right.
hints: Check the symbol: is it > or ≥? Only ≥ includes the boundary.
> means 'more than' — 19 itself does not count, so the circle stays open.

approaching.explanation: x < 5 means 5 is NOT included (open circle) and values less than 5 are shaded (left).
x ≥ 3 means 3 IS included (closed circle) and values greater than or equal to 3 are shaded (right).
x > 8 means 8 is NOT included (open circle) and values greater than 8 are shaded to the right.
x ≤ 10 means 10 IS included (closed circle) and values less than or equal to 10 are shaded (left).

approaching.instructions: Sort each inequality by the type of circle it uses on a number line.
hints: < and > use OPEN circles because the boundary is not included.
≤ and ≥ use CLOSED circles because the boundary IS included.
approaching.label: Sort by circle type: open or closed.

approaching.title: approaching — Spot the Common Mistake

workedExample.label: Read
workedExample.work: A student is working on Graph Inequalities.

workedExample.label: Attempt
workedExample.work: They ignored the rule: Closed circle for ≤ or ≥ (included), open circle for < or > (not included); shade right for greater, left for less.

workedExample.label: Check
workedExample.work: The answer does not match the math rule from class.

approaching.correctWork: Follow the key idea: Closed circle for ≤ or ≥ (included), open circle for < or > (not included); shade right for greater, left for less.
hints: Which step breaks the rule you learned today?
Remember: Closed circle for ≤ or ≥ (included), open circle for < or > (not included); shade right for greater, left for less.

onLevel.label: Match each inequality to its graph description

onLevel.instructions: Read the graph description and write the inequality it represents. Then graph it.

problems.label: Closed circle at 5, shaded right → write the inequality
Open circle at 3, shaded left → write the inequality

hints: Closed circle means ≤ or ≥. Shaded right means greater, so ≥.
Open circle means < or >. Shaded left means less, so <.

onLevel.stem: A number line shows a closed circle at 8 and is shaded to the left. Which inequality does this represent?
onLevel.explanation: Closed circle means the value is included (≤ or ≥). Shaded left means less than or equal to: x ≤ 8.

onLevel.label: Complete the table comparing how different symbols affect the graph.

onLevel.title: onLevel — Spot the Common Mistake

workedExample.label: Read
workedExample.work: A student is working on Graph Inequalities.

workedExample.label: Attempt
workedExample.work: They ignored the rule: Closed circle for ≤ or ≥ (included), open circle for < or > (not included); shade right for greater, left for less.

workedExample.label: Check
workedExample.work: The answer does not match the math rule from class.

onLevel.correctWork: Follow the key idea: Closed circle for ≤ or ≥ (included), open circle for < or > (not included); shade right for greater, left for less.
hints: Which step breaks the rule you learned today?
Remember: Closed circle for ≤ or ≥ (included), open circle for < or > (not included); shade right for greater, left for less.

extending.title: Find the Graphing Error
extending.correctWork: x > 6 uses a strict inequality (no 'equal to'), so the circle should be OPEN, not closed. Open circle at 6, shaded right.
hints: Look at the inequality symbol carefully. Is it > or ≥?
> means strictly greater than — 6 is NOT included, so the circle must be open.

extending.prompt: A theme park has two rides. Ride A requires height h > 48 inches. Ride B requires height h ≥ 48 inches. A child is exactly 48 inches tall. Which ride can they go on? Explain using number line graphs for both inequalities.
extending.sentenceFrame: For Ride A (h > 48), the graph has a ___ circle at 48. Since 48 > 48 is ___, the child ___ ride A. For Ride B (h ≥ 48), the graph has a ___ circle at 48. Since 48 ≥ 48 is ___, the child ___ ride B.

extending.instructions: Graph the compound constraint: the suspect's age a is at least 18 AND at most 35. Graph a ≥ 18 and a ≤ 35 on the same number line. What range of ages is possible?
hints: a ≥ 18: closed circle at 18, shade right. a ≤ 35: closed circle at 35, shade left.
The overlap is the solution: all ages from 18 to 35, inclusive.

practice.commonMistake: A common mistake in Graph Inequalities is skipping the key idea: "Closed circle for ≤ or ≥ (included), open circle for < or > (not included); shade right for greater, left for less." — always check your work against this rule before you submit.

connect.scenario: A detective determines that fewer than 12 people were at the scene. The inequality p < 12 represents the number of people p. She graphs this with an open circle at 12 and shading to the left.
connect.promptQuestion: Why did the detective use an open circle instead of a closed circle? What values of p are possible?
connect.prompt: The detective used an open circle because ___ means 12 is ___ included, so possible values are numbers ___ than 12.

exitTicket.stem: How would you graph the inequality x ≥ 9?
exitTicket.explanation: x ≥ 9 means 9 is included (closed circle) and values are 9 or greater (shaded right).
